1.Introduction & Program Outcomes 
1.1 Introduction This Regulation will govern the provisions for imparting courses of  instructions, conducting 
examinations and evaluation of  students performance leading to a degree in the following disciplines. It 
will be effective for the admission year batch of  2022-23.  
Bachelor of  Technology (B.Tech.) Degree Programs 
1.Civil Engineering  
2.Mechanical Engineering 
3.Electrical Engineering  
4.Electrical and Electronics  Engineering  
5.Electronics and Communication Engineering 
6.Computer Science and Engineering 
7.Computer Science and Engineering (Artiﬁcial Intelligence and Machine Learning) 
8.Computer Science and Engineering (Cyber Security) 
9.Computer Science and Engineering (Data Science) 
10.Computer Science and Engineering (Internet of  Things)

8.Computer Science and Engineering (Cyber Security) 
9.Computer Science and Engineering (Data Science) 
10.Computer Science and Engineering (Internet of  Things) 
11.Computer Science and Information Technology 
The provisions of  this Regulation shall also be applicable to any new discipline that may be 
introduced from time to time and added to the list.  
The Board of  Management may , on the recommendation of  the Academic Council (A.C.), change 
any or all parts of  this Regulation at any time considered appropriate.

2.Academic Calendar 
The academic session is divided into two semesters each of  approximately 20 weeks (21 weeks odd 
semester and 19 weeks even semester) duration: an Autumn (Odd) Semester (September - January) and a 
Spring (Even) Semester (February - May)*.  
The schedule of  academic activities for a session, inclusive of  dates for registration, mid-semester and 
end-semester examinations inter-semester breaks etc., as laid down in the non-negotiable Academic 
Calendar for the session & approved by the Academic Council is to be followed. 
* However, the aforementioned may change in case of  an emergency or under unforeseen circumstances.

Calendar for the session & approved by the Academic Council is to be followed. 
* However, the aforementioned may change in case of  an emergency or under unforeseen circumstances.

3.Admission 
1.Admission to all courses will be made in the First Y ear level, through a National Level Common 
Admission Test (SAAT) conducted by Siksha ‘O’ Anusandhan (Deemed to be University) every year.  
2.Besides the successful SAAT candidates, a speciﬁed % of  students may be admitted to a programme 
through JEE (Main)/other State Level/ National Level Examination as approved by Academic 
Council & Board of  Management.  
3.In special cases the Institute may admit students to a course on transfer from other Institutes. Such 
admission may be made at any level considered appropriate by the Academic Council except at ﬁrst

admission may be made at any level considered appropriate by the Academic Council except at ﬁrst 
year level. Such admission shall be governed by the decision of  the equivalence committee of  the 
(Deemed to be University).  
4.Provisions of  this Regulation do not prevent the (Deemed to be University) from allowing students 
enrolled in a (University/Deemed to be University) in India or abroad to attend speciﬁed subjects in 
one or more semesters without leading to any degree on certain terms and conditions to be speciﬁed 
by the Academic Council and approved by the Board of  Management.  
5.All students admitted to any of  the courses including those accepted under clause 3.4 above shall be 
required to pay at the time of  admission and also in subsequent semesters, prevalent tuition fee and 
other fees as prescribed by the (Deemed to be University) till they are on roll.  
6.The (Deemed to be University) reserves the right to cancel the admission of  any student, and ask

other fees as prescribed by the (Deemed to be University) till they are on roll.  
6.The (Deemed to be University) reserves the right to cancel the admission of  any student, and ask 
him/her to discontinue his/her studies at any stage of  his/her career on grounds of  unsatisfactory 
academic performance, irregular attendance in classes or indiscipline activity .  
7.The (Deemed to be University) reserves the right to ﬁll up the vacant seats, if  any , in any discipline in 
the beginning of  the semester on a suitable basis.  
LATERAL ENTR Y FOR DIPLOMA HOLDERS 
8.Up to a maximum of  10 % of  sanctioned intake in Engineering (over and above, supernumerary to 
the approved intake) would be ﬁlled up through SAAT by candidates satisfying the eligibility criteria 
as set by Siksha ‘O’ Anusandhan for the concerned admission year. The admission will be done to 
the third semester degree courses speciﬁc to the diploma discipline of  the candidate.

as set by Siksha ‘O’ Anusandhan for the concerned admission year. The admission will be done to 
the third semester degree courses speciﬁc to the diploma discipline of  the candidate.

4.Residence Requirements 
1.All students must abide by the rules and regulations of  the Hostel as may be framed from time to 
time.  
2.The Dean/Warden may permit a student to reside with his Parent/Guardian within a reasonable 
distance from the Institute. However, this permission may be withdrawn at the discretion of  the 
Institute, at any time considered appropriate without assigning any reason.  
3.No married accommodation shall be provided to any student of  the undergraduate courses.  
4.No student shall come into or give up the assigned accommodation in any Hostel without the prior 
permission of  the Dean.

4.No student shall come into or give up the assigned accommodation in any Hostel without the prior 
permission of  the Dean.  
5.A student shall reside in a room allotted to him/her and may shift to any other room only under the 
direction/permission of  the Warden.  
6.Students shall be required to make their rooms available whenever required for inspection, repair, 
maintenance or disinfecting and shall vacate the room when leaving for the vacations/holidays.  
7.Students shall be responsible for the proper care of  furniture, fan and other ﬁttings in the rooms 
allotted to them and shall generally assist the Superintendent/Warden in ensuring proper use, care 
and security of  those provided in the Hostels for common use of  all students.  
8.Students will be responsible for the safe keeping of  their own property . In the event of  loss of  any 
personal property of  a student due to theft, ﬁre or any other cause, the Institute shall accept no

personal property of  a student due to theft, ﬁre or any other cause, the Institute shall accept no 
responsibility and shall not be liable for payment of  any compensation.  
9.Engaging personal attendants, keeping pets and use of  appliances like electric heater, refrigerators 
etc., by a student in the Hostel are prohibited.  
10.The mess of  each Hostel shall function as a single integrated unit and shall not, under any 
circumstances be sub-divided into any kind of  groups or sub-groups.  
ADMISSION BATCH 2022 PAGE   OF  6 39ACADEMIC REGULATION FOR PROGRAMS LEADING TO BACHELOR OF TECHNOLOGY DEGREES
5.Registration 
1.Every student of  the B.Tech is required to register at the commencement of  each semester on the 
day ﬁxed for and notiﬁed in the Academic Calendar.  
2.Registration of  students for the First (Autumn) Semester will be done at the time of  admission. For all 
other semesters the registration will be done under the supervision of  the Head of  the Department

other semesters the registration will be done under the supervision of  the Head of  the Department 
through the faculty advisor of  the student. 
3.A student who does not register on the day announced for the purpose may be permitted, in 
consideration of  any compelling reason, late registration within the speci ﬁed working days on 
payment of  late fee as prescribed by the Institute. Normally no late registration shall be permitted 
after 10 days from the scheduled date.  
4.Only those students will be permitted to register who have:  
4.1.Cleared all Institute and Hostel arrear dues of  the previous semesters,  
4.2.Paid all prescribed fees for the current semester, and his / her own faculty advisor Not been 
debarred from registering for a speciﬁed period on disciplinary or any other ground.  
5.A student must satisfy the Promotion criteria set for his/her program. 
5.1.To be able to register in the Second year (at the end of  the First year), then he/she must satisfy

5.A student must satisfy the Promotion criteria set for his/her program. 
5.1.To be able to register in the Second year (at the end of  the First year), then he/she must satisfy 
Promotion Criteria 1. 
5.2.To be able to register in Third year (at the end of  Second year), then he/she must satisfy 
Promotion Criteria 2. 
5.3.To be able to register in Fourth year (at the end of  Third year), then he/she must satisfy 
Promotion Criteria 3. 
6.A student will not be allowed to register in a subject that he/she has secured a passing grade in unless 
it is for grade improvement. In this case, the concerned student has to take permission from the 
concerned student section.

7.Assessment There will be continuous assessment of  a student’s performance throughout the semester. When a 
subject is taught by more than one teacher of  one or more departments, The Subject Development 
Committee will :  
1.prepare the course plan for the subject.  
2.co-ordinate instructions & progress of  teaching in the subject to ensure full coverage of  syllabus.

Committee will :

all sections, review & rationalise in consultation with all teachers and forward them to exam section 
8.Numeric Score 
To arrive at a grade obtained by a student in a particular subject, initially a Numeric Score out of  100 
(out of  40 for internal component and out of  60 for external component) will be decided. This formation 
of  the Numeric Score (out of  100) will depend on the Grading patterns mentioned in Clause 11. Each 
Grading Pattern consists of  differing breakdowns for the various sub components that make up the 
Internal and External Components Of  the Numeric Score.

9.1.2 Relative Grading 
The following Seven Point Scale will be Followed with the following Cutoff  Ranges 
  
The minimum possible cutoff  used for “E” grade is 40 (Internal + External), i.e. if  marks obtained are 
less than 40 (Internal + External) then the student won't be given a "E" grade (or above) in a particular 
instance of  the Subject irrespective of  value of  cutoff  for “E” grade. 
The Relative Grading System will only be applicable for those subjects which follow Grading Patterns 1, 
2, and 6. For Relative grading to be applicable, the number of  students in the subject will need to be at 
least 12. Absolute Grading will be applicable otherwise.

9.2 SGPA A Semester Grade Point Average (SGPA) will be calculated after the end of  every semester. 
where 1 <= i <= n, where n is the number of  subjects in a semester and gi is the grade point (including 
‘F’) obtained by the student in ‘i’ subject and ci is the number of  the credits allotted to ‘i’ subject. 
9.3 CGPA The Cumulative Grade Point Average (CGPA) will be ﬁrst calculated after the end of  the ﬁrst semester 
and will be updated at the end of  every semester till graduation.

and will be updated at the end of  every semester till graduation.  
where 1 <= i <= n, where n is the total number of  subjects taken by the student from the ﬁrst semester 
till the current semester and gi is the grade point obtained by the student in ‘i’ subject (including ‘F’) and 
ci is the number of  the credits allotted to ‘i’ subject. 
10.Graduation CGPA Requirements The Minimum CGPA Required for Graduation  
The Minimum Cumulative Grade Point Average required for Graduation is 6.0  i.e. a 
Student can only be considered for graduation if  and only if  his/her Cumulative Grade Point Average 
(after complying with all the requirements of  the (Deemed to be University) as mentioned in the clause 14 regarding 
Graduation Requirements of  the academic regulation and those required by the Constituent College for graduation ) is

Graduation Requirements of  the academic regulation and those required by the Constituent College for graduation ) is 
greater than or equal to 6.0 (six point zero).

16.‘F’ Grade 
A ‘F’ grade will be awarded to the student if  he/she falls within any of  the following 
•Fails to satisfy the requirements for a passing grade in a  subject 
•Fails to satisfy the requirements for appearing the external exam of  a  subject. 
•Is caught indulging in plagiarism three times during the duration of  the particular subject (either 
during assignments, labs, projects or during exams)

•Is caught indulging in plagiarism three times during the duration of  the particular subject (either 
during assignments, labs, projects or during exams) 
•Is involved in a disciplinary act where the Standing Committee on Student Discipline & Welfare deems 
it necessary to award the student with ‘F’ grade 
If  the student is awarded an ‘F’ grade in a subject, then it counts as a backlog paper. In this case, he/she 
must retake the subject in its entirety . Once the Student has obtained a Passing grade in the Subject, the 
‘F’ grade obtained previously shall be replaced by the Passing Grade obtained thus. The CGPA of  the 
student will be updated accordingly . 
If  the Student is unable to secure a passing grade, then he/she must register for the subject (whenever it 
is offered next), take the subject again in its entirety again and hope to secure a passing grade.

19.Regulation for Branch Change 1.A student admitted to a particular branch of  the B.Tech. (Engineering, Management) Course will 
normally continue studying in that branch till completion.  
2.However, in special cases the Institute may permit a student to change from one branch of  studies to 
another after the ﬁrst two semesters. Such changes will be permitted, strictly in accordance with the 
provisions laid down hereinafter.  
3.Only those students will be eligible for consideration for a change of  branch after the Second 
(Spring) Semester, who have –  
1. Completed all the credits prescribed in the ﬁrst two semesters of  their studies, in their ﬁrst attempt, 
without having had to pass any course requirement in the supplementary examination and/or 
summer quarter.

without having had to pass any course requirement in the supplementary examination and/or

satisfy the Promotion Criteria 1 for Branch B, then the credit hours of  that subject will be 
added to total credit hours of  the student. 
3. Obtained a CGPA not lower than 9.0 , at the end of  the Second (Spring) Semester.

prescribed for this purpose. The Dean (ITER) will call for application sometime in the Spring

CONDUCT AND DISCIPLINE 
With a view to keep the reputation & image of  Siksha ‘O’ Anusandhan (Deemed to be University) high, 
the students of  the (Deemed to be University) are required to maintain discipline in the campus and are 
required to; 
1.Show due respect to their teachers, Warden & Superintendents of  Hostels and show proper courtesy 
to other members of  the staff  of  the (Deemed to be University).  
2.Maintain good rapport and friendly relationship with fellow students;  
3.Behave affectionately with the new students admitted every year.  
4.Do not indulge in any acts of  ragging. Ragging in any form is banned by law . Any Act of  physical or 
mental harassment to junior students individually or in group, is considered as an Act of  ragging and

mental harassment to junior students individually or in group, is considered as an Act of  ragging and 
calls for strict disciplinary actions including expulsion from the (Deemed to be University) and Police 
action. Hon’ble Supreme Court’s decisions on ragging and subsequent directions from UGC are to 
be strictly adhered to.

outside the campus.  
8.Wilfully damaging or stealthily removing any property belonging to the (Deemed to be University)/

Spring (Even) Semester (February - May)*.  
The schedule of  academic activities for a session, inclusive of  dates for registration, mid-semester and 
end-semester examinations inter-semester breaks etc., as laid down in the non-negotiable Academic 
Calendar for the session & approved by the Academic Council is to be followed.

4.Residence Requirements 
1.All students must abide by the rules and regulations of  the Hostel as may be framed from time to 
time.  
2.The Dean/Warden may permit a student to reside with his Parent/Guardian within a reasonable 
distance from the Institute. However, this permission may be withdrawn at the discretion of  the 
Institute, at any time considered appropriate without assigning any reason.  
3.No married accommodation shall be provided to any student of  the undergraduate courses.  
4.No student shall come into or give up the assigned accommodation in any Hostel without the prior 
permission of  the Dean.

4.No student shall come into or give up the assigned accommodation in any Hostel without the prior 
permission of  the Dean.  
5.A student shall reside in a room allotted to him/her and may shift to any other room only under the 
direction/permission of  the Warden.  
6.Students shall be required to make their rooms available whenever required for inspection, repair, 
maintenance or disinfecting and shall vacate the room when leaving for the vacations/holidays.  
7.Students shall be responsible for the proper care of  furniture, fan and other ﬁttings in the rooms 
allotted to them and shall generally assist the Superintendent/Warden in ensuring proper use, care

16.‘F’ Grade A ‘F’ grade will be awarded to the student if  he/she falls within any of  the following

16.‘F’ Grade A ‘F’ grade will be awarded to the student if  he/she falls within any of  the following 
•Fails to satisfy the requirements for a passing grade in a  subject 
•Fails to satisfy the requirements for appearing the external exam of  a  subject. 
•Is caught indulging in plagiarism three times during the duration of  the particular subject (either 
during assignments, labs, projects or during exams) 
•Is involved in a disciplinary act where the Standing Committee on Student Discipline & Welfare deems 
it necessary to award the student with ‘F’ grade 
If  the student is awarded an ‘F’ grade in a subject, then it counts as a backlog paper. In this case, he/she 
must retake the subject in its entirety . Once the Student has obtained a Passing grade in the Subject, the 
‘F’ grade obtained previously shall be replaced by the Passing Grade obtained thus. The CGPA of  the 
student will be updated accordingly .

29.Grade Improvement 
1.Students securing an ‘E’ grade in a subject may apply for grade improvement to a ‘D’ grade. They 
may appear for grade improvement in a subject for a maximum of  two times.  
1.1.Students may appear for grade improvement in a subject when the subject is offered, either 
during the odd or even semesters or during the summer quarter.   
1.1.1.if  students appear for grade improvement during the even or odd semester, the grading 
system that will be followed will be dependent on the grading pattern of  the subject 
1.1.2.if  students appear for grade improvement during the summer quarter, the grading system

system that will be followed will be dependent on the grading pattern of  the subject 
1.1.2.if  students appear for grade improvement during the summer quarter, the grading system 
that will be followed will be the absolute grading system, irrespective of  the grading pattern 
of  the subject 
2.To register for grade improvement in a subject,  
2.1.students will, ﬁrstly , be required to get permission from the controller of  examinations. 
2.2.Once, they have secured permission from the controller of  examinations, they will have to ﬁnalise 
registration by depositing an amount of  INR 4000 per subject. 
3.To satisfactorily complete grade improvement in a subject, students will be required 
3.1.secure a passing grade in the subject by completing the subject by attending classes and by 
completing the internal and external requirements of  the subject. 
3.2.the secured passing grade will need to be be better than a ‘D’ or better for them to upgrade their

completing the internal and external requirements of  the subject. 
3.2.the secured passing grade will need to be be better than a ‘D’ or better for them to upgrade their 
grade in the subject from an ‘E’ to a ‘D’. 
4.If  the secured passing grade is not an improvement upon their grade in their previous attempt, the 
older grade (‘E’ grade) will hold.

SIKSHA ‘O’ ANUSANDHANAcademic Regulation For Program Leading to Bachelor of Computer Applications Degree
1. Introduction & Program Outcomes
1.1 Introduction
1. This Regulation will govern the provisions for imparting courses of instructions, conducting exami-
nations and evaluation of students performance in 3-years course leading to Bachelor of Computer
Applications (BCA). It will be e ﬀective for the admission year batches of 2024 and 2025.
2. The Board of Management (B.M.) may , on the recommendation of the Academic Council (A.C.),
change any or all parts of the Regulation at any time considered appropriate.
Admission Batches 2024 and 2025 P AGE 1 of 29Academic Regulation For Program Leading to Bachelor of Computer Applications Degree
1.2 Program Outcomes
The BCA program o ﬀered at ITER, Siksha ‘O’ Anusandhan (Deemed to be University) need to satisfy the
following program outcomes

1.2 Program Outcomes
The BCA program o ﬀered at ITER, Siksha ‘O’ Anusandhan (Deemed to be University) need to satisfy the
following program outcomes
1. Computational Knowledge: Apply knowledge of computing fundamentals, computing specializa-
tion, mathematics, and domain knowledge appropriate for the computing specialization to the ab-
straction and conceptualization of computing models from de ﬁned problems and requirements.
2. Problem Analysis: Identify , formulate, research literature, and solve complex computing problems
reaching substantiated conclusions using fundamental principles of mathematics, computing sciences,
and relevant domain disciplines.
3. Design /Development of Solutions: Design and evaluate solutions for complex computing problems,
and design and evaluate systems, components, or processes that meet speci ﬁed needs with appropriate
consideration for public health and safety , cultural, societal, and environmental considerations.

and design and evaluate systems, components, or processes that meet speci ﬁed needs with appropriate
consideration for public health and safety , cultural, societal, and environmental considerations.
4. Conduct investigations of complex computing problems: Use research-based knowledge and
research methods including design of experiments, analysis and interpretation of data, and synthesis
of the information to provide valid conclusions.
5. Modern T ool Usage: Create, select, adapt and apply appropriate techniques, resources, and modern
computing tools to complex computing activities, with an understanding of the limitations.
6. Professional Ethics: Understand and commit to professional ethics and cyber regulations, responsi-
bilities, and norms of professional computing practices.
7. Life-long Learning: Recognize the need, and have the ability , to engage in independent learning for
continual development as a computing professional.

7. Life-long Learning: Recognize the need, and have the ability , to engage in independent learning for
continual development as a computing professional.
8. Project management and ﬁnance: Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the computing
and management principles and apply these to one’s own work, as a member and leader in a team,
to manage projects and in multidisciplinary environments.
9. Communication Eﬃcacy: Communicate eﬀectively with the computing community , and with society
at large, about complex computing activities by being able to comprehend and write e ﬀective reports,
design documentation, make e ﬀective presentations, and give and understand clear instructions.
10. Societal and Environmental Concern: Understand and assess societal, environmental, health,
safety , legal, and cultural issues within local and global contexts, and the consequential responsi-
bilities relevant to professional computing practices.

safety , legal, and cultural issues within local and global contexts, and the consequential responsi-
bilities relevant to professional computing practices.
11. Individual and T eam W ork: Function e ﬀectively as an individual and as a member or leader in
diverse teams and in multidisciplinary environments.
12. Innovation and Entrepreneurship: Identify a timely opportunity and using innovation to pursue
that opportunity to create value and wealth for the betterment of the individual and society at large.

and 19 weeks even semester) duration: an Autumn (Odd) Semester (September - January) and a Spring
(Even) Semester (February - May)∗.

and 19 weeks even semester) duration: an Autumn (Odd) Semester (September - January) and a Spring

Such admission may be made at any level considered appropriate by the Academic Council except
at ﬁrst year level. Such admission shall be governed by the decision of the equivalence committee of

required to pay at the time of admission and also in subsequent semesters, prevalent tuition fee and

6. The (Deemed to be University) reserves the right to cancel the admission of any student, and ask
him/her to discontinue his/her studies at any stage of his/her career on grounds of unsatisfactory
academic performance, irregular attendance in classes or indiscipline activity.

the semester on a suitable basis.

4. Residence Requirements
1. All students must abide by the rules and regulations of the Hostel as may be framed from time to
time.
2. The Dean/Warden may permit a student to reside with his Parent/Guardian within a reasonable
distance from the Institute. However, this permission may be withdrawn at the discretion of the
Institute, at any time considered appropriate without assigning any reason.
3. No married accommodation shall be provided to any student of the undergraduate courses.
4. No student shall come into or give up the assigned accommodation in any Hostel without the prior
permission of the Dean.
5. A student shall reside in a room allotted to him/her and may shift to any other room only under the
direction/permission of the Warden.

permission of the Dean.
5. A student shall reside in a room allotted to him/her and may shift to any other room only under the
direction/permission of the Warden.
6. Students shall be required to make their rooms available whenever required for inspection, repair, and
maintenance or disinfecting and shall vacate the room when leaving for the vacations/holidays.
7. Students shall be responsible for the proper care of furniture, fan and other ﬁttings in the rooms
allotted to them and shall generally assist the Superintendent/Warden in ensuring proper use, care
and security of those provided in the Hostels for common use of all students.
8. Students will be responsible for the safekeeping of their own property. In the event of the loss of
any personal property of a student due to theft, ﬁre or any other cause, the Institute shall accept no
responsibility and shall not be liable for payment of any compensation.

any personal property of a student due to theft, ﬁre or any other cause, the Institute shall accept no

9. Engaging personal attendants, keeping pets and use of appliances like electric heater, refrigerators
etc., by a student in the Hostel are prohibited.
10. The mess of each Hostel shall function as a single integrated unit and shall not, under any circum-
stances be sub-divided into any kind of groups or sub-groups.

1. Every student of the BCA is required to register at the commencement of each semester on the day
ﬁxed for and noti ﬁed in the Academic Calendar.

all other semesters the registration will be done under the supervision of the Head of the Department

late fee as prescribed by the Institute. Normally no late registration shall be permitted after 10 days
from the scheduled date.

6. Promotion Criteria
From First year to Second Y ear: For BCA students, to be promoted from the First Y ear to the Second
Y ear, a student must pass in at least 24 credits of the 1 st year (1st & 2 nd semester taken together) course.
From Second Y ear to Third Y ear:For BCA students, to be promoted from the Second Y ear to the Third
Y ear, a student must pass in at least 48 credits in total. This is inclusive of 24 credits of 1 st year (1 st & 2 nd
semester taken together) course and 24 credits of the 2 nd year (3rd & 4 th semester taken together) course.

There will be continuous assessment of a student’s performance throughout the semester. When a subject

7. Assessment
There will be continuous assessment of a student’s performance throughout the semester. When a subject
is taught by more than one teacher of one or more departments, The Subject Development Committee will:
1. Prepare the course plan for the subject.
2. Co-ordinate instructions & progress of teaching in the subject to ensure full coverage of syllabus.
3. Set questions for internal test in consultation with the teachers. Compile internal assessment marks of
all sections, review & rationalize in consultation with all teachers and forward them to exam section.

16. ‘F’ Grade
A ‘F’ grade will be awarded to the student if he/she falls within any of the following
• Fails to satisfy the requirements for a passing grade in a subject
• Fails to satisfy the requirements for appearing the external exam of a subject.
• Is caught indulging in plagiarism three times during the duration of the particular subject (either
during assignments, labs, projects or during exams)
• Is involved in a disciplinary act where the Standing Committee on Student Discipline & Welfare deems
it necessary to award the student with ‘F’ grade

• Is involved in a disciplinary act where the Standing Committee on Student Discipline & Welfare deems
it necessary to award the student with ‘F’ grade
If the student is awarded an ‘F’ grade in a subject, then it counts as a backlog paper. In this case,
he/she must retake the subject in its entirety. Once the Student has obtained a Passing grade in the
Subject, the ‘F’ grade obtained previously shall be replaced by the Passing Grade obtained thus. The
CGP A of the student will be updated accordingly.
If the Student is unable to secure a passing grade, then he/she must register for the subject (whenever
it is o ﬀered next), take the subject again in its entirety again and hope to secure a passing grade.

CONDUCT AND DISCIPLINE
With a view to keep the reputation & image of Siksha ‘O’ Anusandhan (Deemed to be University) high, the
students of the (Deemed to be University) are required to maintain discipline in the campus and are required
to;
1. Show due respect to their teachers, Warden & Superintendents of Hostels and show proper courtesy
to other members of the sta ﬀ of the (Deemed to be University).
2. Maintain good rapport and friendly relationship with fellow students.
3. Behave a ﬀectionately with the new students admitted every year.
4. Do not indulge in any acts of ragging. Ragging in any form is banned by law. Any Act of physical or
mental harassment to junior students individually or in group, is considered as an Act of ragging and
calls for strict disciplinary actions including expulsion from the (Deemed to be University) and Police

calls for strict disciplinary actions including expulsion from the (Deemed to be University) and Police
action. Hon’ble Supreme Court’s decisions on ragging and subsequent directions from UGC are to be
strictly adhered to.

5. Furnishing false statement of any kind for admission or for award of prizes etc.
6. Furnishing false statement to the Disciplinary Committee, or willfully withholding information relevant
to an enquiry.
7. Displaying lack of courtesy and decorum; resorting to indecent behaviour anywhere within or outside
the campus.
8. Willfully damaging or stealthily removing any property belonging to the (Deemed to be Univer-
sity)/Institutions/ Hostels or of fellow students.
9. Possession, consumption or distribution of narcotic products and alcoholic drinks or drug.

27. Grade Improvement
1. Students securing an ‘E’ grade in a subject may apply for grade improvement to a ‘D’ grade. They
may appear for grade improvement in a subject for a maximum of two times.
1.1. Students may appear for grade improvement in a subject when the subject is o ﬀered, either
during the odd or even semesters or during the summer quarter.
1.1.1. students appear for grade improvement during the even or odd semester, the grading system
that will be followed will be dependent on the grading pattern of the subject
1.1.2. if students appear for grade improvement during the summer quarter, the grading system
that will be followed will be the absolute grading system, irrespective of the grading pattern
of the subject
2. T o register for grade improvement in a subject,
2.1. students will, ﬁrstly , be required to get permission from the controller of examinations.
2.2. Once, they have secured permission from the controller of examinations, they will have to ﬁnalise

2.1. students will, ﬁrstly , be required to get permission from the controller of examinations.
2.2. Once, they have secured permission from the controller of examinations, they will have to ﬁnalise
registration by depositing an amount of INR 4000 per subject.
3. T o satisfactorily complete grade improvement in a subject, students will be required
3.1. secure a passing grade in the subject by completing the subject by attending classes and by
completing the internal and external requirements of the subject.
3.2. the secured passing grade will need to be be better than a ‘D’ or better for them to upgrade their
grade in the subject from an ‘E’ to a ‘D’.
4. If the secured passing grade is not an improvement upon their grade in their previous attempt, the
older grade (‘E’ grade) will hold.

Program Educational Objectives
PEO 1 Our Graduates will have successful professional careers in industry, government, academia
or non-profit organizations.
PEO 2 Our Graduates will effectively lead, work and communicate in multidisciplinary teams and
apply sound engineering principles and design methodology to solve societal problems.
PEO 3 Our Graduates will maintain currency in their chosen field through higher study, through
organizational participation and through participation in professional developmental activi-

Program Outcomes
PO 1 Graduates will possess in-depth knowledge of core areas in computer science, including algorithms, data structures,
software engineering, database systems, artificial intelligence, and machine learning, and will be able to apply this
knowledge to solve real-world problems.
PO 2 Graduates will be capable of analyzing complex problems, identifying appropriate solutions, and implementing effec-
tive algorithms and methodologies to address computing challenges.
PO 3 Graduates will demonstrate the ability to design, develop, and evaluate software systems or applications, adhering to
industry best practices and design principles, ensuring quality and scalability.

industry best practices and design principles, ensuring quality and scalability.
PO 4 Graduates will possess the skills to conduct independent research, contribute original ideas, and engage in innovation
within the field, addressing contemporary challenges through novel approaches and technologies.
PO 5 Graduates will be proficient in using contemporary tools, frameworks, and programming languages for software de-
velopment, data analysis, and system design, ensuring effective implementation of solutions.
PO 6 Graduates will understand the principles of data management and analytics, enabling them to collect, analyze, and
interpret data to make informed decisions and derive insights.
PO 7 Graduates will adhere to ethical standards and professional responsibilities in their practice, considering the societal
implications of technology and ensuring the integrity and security of systems and data.

implications of technology and ensuring the integrity and security of systems and data.
PO 8 Graduates will possess the ability to work collaboratively in teams, demonstrating leadership in project management,
coordinating efforts, and fostering a productive team environment.
PO 9 Graduates will be able to communicate complex technical concepts and solutions clearly and effectively to diverse
audiences, both in written and oral formats, including reports, presentations, and documentation.
PO 10 Graduates will acquire project management skills, enabling them to plan, execute, and manage software projects effi-
ciently, and will develop an entrepreneurial mindset to innovate and lead technology-driven initiatives.
PO 11 Graduates will recognize the importance of lifelong learning and professional development to stay current with
emerging technologies, trends, and practices in computer science.

SIKSHA ‘O’ ANUSANDHANAcademic Regulation For Program Leading to Master of Computer Applications Degree
1. Introduction & Program Outcomes
1.1 Introduction
1. This Regulation will govern the provisions for imparting instructions, conducting examinations and
evaluation of students’ performance in 2-years course leading to Master of Computer Application (MCA).
It will be e ﬀective for the admission year batch of 2025.
2. The Board of Management (B.M.) may , on the recommendation of the Academic Council (A.C.) , change
any or all parts of the Regulation at any time considered appropriate.
2025 Admission Batch P AGE 1 of 32Academic Regulation For Program Leading to Master of Computer Applications Degree
1.2 Program Outcomes
PO1 - Foundation Knowledge: Apply knowledge of mathematics, programming logic and coding funda-
mentals for solution architecture and problem solving.

1.2 Program Outcomes
PO1 - Foundation Knowledge: Apply knowledge of mathematics, programming logic and coding funda-
mentals for solution architecture and problem solving.
PO2 - Problem Analysis: Identify , review, formulate and analyse problems for primarily focussing on
customer requirements using critical thinking frameworks.
PO3 - Development of Solutions: Design, develop and investigate problems with as an innovative ap-
proach for solutions incorporating ESG/SDG goals.
PO4 - Modern T ool Usage: Select, adapt and apply modern computational tools such as development of
algorithms with an understanding of the limitations including human biases.
PO5 - Individual and T eamwork: Function and communicate eﬀectively as an individual or a team leader
in diverse and multidisciplinary groups. Use methodologies such as agile.
PO6 - Project Management and Finance: Use the principles of project management such as scheduling,

in diverse and multidisciplinary groups. Use methodologies such as agile.
PO6 - Project Management and Finance: Use the principles of project management such as scheduling,
work breakdown structure and be conversant with the principles of Finance for pro ﬁtable project manage-
ment.
PO7 - Ethics: Commit to professional ethics in managing software projects with ﬁnancial aspects. Learn
to use new technologies for cyber security and insulate customers from malware.
PO8 - Life-long learning: Change management skills and the ability to learn, keep up with contemporary
technologies and ways of working.
2025 Admission Batch P AGE 2 of 32Academic Regulation For Program Leading to Master of Computer Applications Degree
2. Academic Calendar
The academic session is divided into two semesters each of approximately 20 weeks (21 weeks odd semester
and 19 weeks even semester) duration: an Autumn (Odd) Semester (September - January) and a Spring
(Even) Semester (February - May)∗.

and 19 weeks even semester) duration: an Autumn (Odd) Semester (September - January) and a Spring
(Even) Semester (February - May)∗.
The schedule of academic activities for a session, inclusive of dates for registration, mid-semester and
end-semester examinations inter-semester breaks etc., as laid down in the non-negotiable Academic Calen-
dar for the session & approved by the Academic Council is to be followed.
∗ This timeline may change with due approval from concerned SOA authorities .
2025 Admission Batch P AGE 3 of 32Academic Regulation For Program Leading to Master of Computer Applications Degree
3. Admission
1. Admission to MCA program will be made in the First Y ear level, through a National Level Common
Admission T est (SAA T) conducted by Siksha ‘O’ Anusandhan (Deemed to be University) every year.
2. Besides the successful SAA T candidates, a speci ﬁed % of students may be admitted to MCA program

Admission T est (SAA T) conducted by Siksha ‘O’ Anusandhan (Deemed to be University) every year.
2. Besides the successful SAA T candidates, a speci ﬁed % of students may be admitted to MCA program
through other State Level / National Level Examination as approved by Academic Council & Board of
Management.
3. In special cases the Institute may admit students to MCA program on transfer from other Institutes. Such
admission may be made at any level considered appropriate by the Academic Council except at ﬁrst year
level. Such admission shall be governed by the decision of the equivalence committee of SOA (Deemed
to be University).
4. Provisions of this Regulation do not prevent the (Deemed to be University) from allowing students enrolled
in a (University/Deemed to be University) in India or abroad to attend speci ﬁed subjects in one or more
semesters without leading to any degree on certain terms and conditions to be speci ﬁed by the Academic

semesters without leading to any degree on certain terms and conditions to be speci ﬁed by the Academic
Council and approved by the Board of Management.
5. All students admitted to the course including those accepted under clause 3.4 above shall be required to
pay at the time of admission and also in subsequent semesters, prevalent tuition fee and other fees as
prescribed by the (Deemed to be University) till they are on roll.
6. The (Deemed to be University) reserves the right to cancel the admission of any student, and ask him/her
to discontinue his/her studies at any stage of his/her career on grounds of unsatisfactory academic
performance, irregular attendance in classes or indiscipline activity.
7. The (Deemed to be University) reserves the right to ﬁll up the vacant seats, if any , in the beginning of the
semester on a suitable basis.

4. Residence Requirements
1. All students must abide by the rules and regulations of the Hostel as may be framed from time to time.
2. The Dean/Warden may permit a student to reside with his Parent/Guardian within a reasonable distance
from the Institute. However, this permission may be withdrawn at the discretion of the Institute, at any
time considered appropriate without assigning any reason.
3. No married accommodation shall be provided to any student of the undergraduate courses.
4. No student shall come into or give up the assigned accommodation in any Hostel without the prior
permission of the Dean.
5. A student shall reside in a room allotted to him/her and may shift to any other room only under the
direction/permission of the Warden.

permission of the Dean.
5. A student shall reside in a room allotted to him/her and may shift to any other room only under the
direction/permission of the Warden.
6. Students shall be required to make their rooms available whenever required for inspection, repair, and
maintenance or disinfecting and shall vacate the room when leaving for the vacations/holidays.
7. Students shall be responsible for the proper care of furniture, fan and other ﬁttings in the rooms allotted
to them and shall generally assist the Superintendent/Warden in ensuring proper use, care and security
of those provided in the Hostels for common use of all students.
8. Students will be responsible for the safekeeping of their own property. In the event of the loss of
any personal property of a student due to theft, ﬁre or any other cause, the Institute shall accept no
responsibility and shall not be liable for payment of any compensation.

any personal property of a student due to theft, ﬁre or any other cause, the Institute shall accept no
responsibility and shall not be liable for payment of any compensation.
9. Engaging personal attendants, keeping pets and use of appliances like electric heater, refrigerators etc.,
by a student in the Hostel are prohibited.
10. The mess of each Hostel shall function as a single integrated unit and shall not, under any circumstances
be sub-divided into any kind of groups or sub-groups.
2025 Admission Batch P AGE 5 of 32Academic Regulation For Program Leading to Master of Computer Applications Degree
5. Registration
1. Every student of the MCA is required to register at the commencement of each semester on the day ﬁxed
for and noti ﬁed in the Academic Calendar.
2. Registration of students for the First (Autumn) Semester will be done at the time of admission. For
all other semesters the registration will be done under the supervision of the Head of the Department

all other semesters the registration will be done under the supervision of the Head of the Department
through the faculty advisor of the student.
3. A student who does not register on the day announced for the purpose may be permitted, in consideration
of any compelling reason, late registration within the speci ﬁed working days on payment of late fee as
prescribed by the Institute. Normally no late registration shall be permitted after 10 days from the
scheduled date.
4. Only those students will be permitted to register who have:
4.1. Cleared all Institute and Hostel dues of the previous semesters,
4.2. Paid all prescribed fees for the current semester, and not been debarred from registering for a
speciﬁed period on disciplinary or any other ground.
5. T o be able to register in the second year at the end of the ﬁrst year a student must pass in at least 24
Credits of the 1 st year (1st & 2 nd semester taken together) course.

5. T o be able to register in the second year at the end of the ﬁrst year a student must pass in at least 24
Credits of the 1 st year (1st & 2 nd semester taken together) course.
6. A student will not be allowed to register in a subject that he/she has secured a passing grade in unless it
is for grade improvement. In this case, the concerned student has to take permission from the concerned
student section.
2025 Admission Batch P AGE 6 of 32Academic Regulation For Program Leading to Master of Computer Applications Degree
6. Promotion Criteria
From First year to Second Y ear: For MCA students, to be promoted from the First Y ear to the Second
Y ear, a student must pass in at least 24 credits of the 1st year (1st & 2nd semester taken together) course.
7. Assessment
There will be continuous assessment of a student’s performance throughout the semester. When a subject
is taught by more than one teacher of one or more departments, The Subject Development Committee will:

is taught by more than one teacher of one or more departments, The Subject Development Committee will:

2. Co-ordinate instructions & progress of teaching in the subject to ensure full coverage of syllabus.
3. Set questions for internal test in consultation with the teachers. compile internal assessment marks of all
sections, review & rationalize in consultation with all teachers and forward them to exam section.
8. Numeric Score
T o arrive at a grade obtained by a student in a particular subject, initially a Numeric Score out of 100 (out

Numeric Score (out of 100) will depend on the Grading patterns mentioned in Clause 10. Each Grading
Pattern consists of di ﬀering breakdowns for the various sub components that make up the Internal and
External Components of the Numeric Score.

15. ‘F’ Grade
A ‘F’ grade will be awarded to the student if he/she falls within any of the following
• Fails to satisfy the requirements for a passing grade in a subject
• Fails to satisfy the requirements for appearing the external exam of a subject.
• Is caught indulging in plagiarism three times during the duration of the particular subject (either during
assignments, labs, projects or during exams)
• Is involved in a disciplinary act where the Standing Committee on Student Discipline & Welfare deems it

assignments, labs, projects or during exams)
• Is involved in a disciplinary act where the Standing Committee on Student Discipline & Welfare deems it
necessary to award the student with ‘F’ grade
If the student is awarded an ‘F’ grade in a subject, then it counts as a backlog paper. In this case, he/she
must retake the subject in its entirety. Once the Student has obtained a Passing grade in the Subject, the ‘F’
grade obtained previously shall be replaced by the Passing Grade obtained thus. The CGP A of the student
will be updated accordingly.
If the Student is unable to secure a passing grade, then he/she must register for the subject (whenever it is
oﬀered next), take the subject again in its entirety again and hope to secure a passing grade.

21. T emporary Withdrawal from the Institute
1. A student who has been admitted to MCA course of the (Deemed to be University) may be permitted to
withdraw temporarily for a period of one year from the programme on grounds of prolonged illness or
acute problem in the family which compelled him/her to stay at home.
2. A student is not allowed to skip one semester and take admission in the higher semester for any reason as
stated above or due to Rustication. Skipping one semester amounts to withdrawal for one year. He/She
will be permitted to take re-admission at the point from where he/she has been allowed temporary
withdrawal, provided;

will be permitted to take re-admission at the point from where he/she has been allowed temporary
withdrawal, provided;
2.1. That he/she applies to the Dean within 15 days of the commencement of the semester or from the
date of illness, stating fully the reasons for such withdrawal together with supporting documents
and endorsement of the parents/ guardian.
2.2. That inclusive of the period of withdrawal, the student is likely to complete the requirements for the
degree within the time limits as speci ﬁed earlier (in Degree Requirements).
2.3. That there are no outstanding dues towards Hostel/ Department/ Library/Sports/ NCC/ NSS etc.
3. Skipping one semester amounts to loss of one year. The student will not be allowed to make up the loss
in summer quarter. He has to repeat the subject in a regular semester as year back.

22. Relaxation
The Academic Council may , under exceptional circumstances, consider any case of a student and relax
the relevant provision of these Regulations based on the merit of the case. The grounds on which such
relaxation is granted shall invariably be recorded and cannot be cited as precedence.

of one or more Departments. The SDC shall be in e ﬀect every semester/summer quarter that the subject is
oﬀered in and will be responsible for performing their functions during this period.

of the subject.

26. Grade Improvement
1. Students securing an ‘E’ grade in a subject may apply for grade improvement to a ‘D’ grade. They may

26. Grade Improvement
1. Students securing an ‘E’ grade in a subject may apply for grade improvement to a ‘D’ grade. They may
appear for grade improvement in a subject for a maximum of two times.
1.1. Students may appear for grade improvement in a subject when the subject is o ﬀered, either during
the odd or even semesters or during the summer quarter.
1.1.1. if students appear for grade improvement during the even or odd semester, the grading system
that will be followed will be dependent on the grading pattern of the subject
1.1.2. if students appear for grade improvement during the summer quarter, the grading system that
will be followed will be the absolute grading system, irrespective of the grading pattern of the
subject
2. T o register for grade improvement in a subject,
2.1. students will, ﬁrstly , be required to get permission from the controller of examinations.
2.2. Once, they have secured permission from the controller of examinations, they will have to ﬁnalise

2.1. students will, ﬁrstly , be required to get permission from the controller of examinations.
2.2. Once, they have secured permission from the controller of examinations, they will have to ﬁnalise
registration by depositing an amount of INR 4000 per subject.
3. T o satisfactorily complete grade improvement in a subject, students will be required
3.1. secure a passing grade in the subject by completing the subject by attending classes and by com-
pleting the internal and external requirements of the subject.
3.2. the secured passing grade will need to be be better than a ‘D’ or better for them to upgrade their
grade in the subject from an ‘E’ to a ‘D’.
4. If the secured passing grade is not an improvement upon their grade in their previous attempt, the older
grade (‘E’ grade) will hold.

Program Outcomes
PO 1 Computational Knowledge: Apply knowledge of computing fundamentals, computing specialization, mathemat-
ics, and domain knowledge appropriate for the computing specialization to the abstraction and conceptualization of
computing models from defined problems and requirements.
PO 2 Problem Analysis: Identify, formulate, research literature, and solve complex computing problems reaching sub-
stantiated conclusions using fundamental principles of mathematics, computing sciences, and relevant domain disci-
plines.
PO 3 Design /Development of Solutions: Design and evaluate solutions for complex computing problems, and design
and evaluate systems, components, or processes that meet specified needs with appropriate consideration for public
health and safety, cultural, societal, and environmental considerations.
PO 4 Conduct investigations of complex computing problems: Use research-based knowledge and research methods

health and safety, cultural, societal, and environmental considerations.
PO 4 Conduct investigations of complex computing problems: Use research-based knowledge and research methods
including design of experiments, analysis and interpretation of data, and synthesis of the information to provide valid
conclusions.
PO 5 Modern Tool Usage: Create, select, adapt and apply appropriate techniques, resources, and modern computing tools
to complex computing activities, with an understanding of the limitations.
PO 6 Professional Ethics: Understand and commit to professional ethics and cyber regulations, responsibilities, and
norms of professional computing practices.
PO 7 Life-long Learning: Recognize the need, and have the ability, to engage in independent learning for continual de-
velopment as a computing professional.
PO 8 Project management and finance: Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the computing and management

velopment as a computing professional.
PO 8 Project management and finance: Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the computing and management
principles and apply these to one’s own work, as a member and leader in a team, to manage projects and in multidis-
ciplinary environments.
PO 9 Communication Efficacy: Communicate effectively with the computing community, and with society at large,
about complex computing activities by being able to comprehend and write effective reports, design documentation,
make effective presentations, and give and understand clear instructions.
PO 10 Societal and Environmental Concern: Understand and assess societal, environmental, health, safety, legal, and
cultural issues within local and global contexts, and the consequential responsibilities relevant to professional com-
puting practices.

PO 1 Foundation Knowledge: Apply knowledge of mathematics, programming logic and coding fundamentals for solu-

Program Outcomes
PO 1 Foundation Knowledge: Apply knowledge of mathematics, programming logic and coding fundamentals for solu-
tion architecture and problem solving.
PO 2 Problem Analysis: Identify, review, formulate and analyse problems for primarily focussing on customer require-
ments using critical thinking frameworks.
PO 3 Development of Solutions: Design, develop and investigate problems with as an innovative approach for solutions
incorporating ESG/SDG goals.
PO 4 Modern Tool Usage: Select, adapt and apply modern computational tools such as development of algorithms with an
understanding of the limitations including human biases.
PO 5 Individual and Teamwork: Function and communicate effectively as an individual or a team leader in diverse and
multidisciplinary groups. Use methodologies such as agile.
PO 6 Project Management and Finance: Use the principles of project management such as scheduling, work breakdown

multidisciplinary groups. Use methodologies such as agile.
PO 6 Project Management and Finance: Use the principles of project management such as scheduling, work breakdown
structure and be conversant with the principles of Finance for profitable project management.
PO 7 Ethics: Commit to professional ethics in managing software projects with financial aspects. Learn to use new tech-
nologies for cyber security and insulate customers from malware.
PO 8 Life-long learning: Change management skills and the ability to learn, keep up with contemporary technologies and
ways of working.Admission Batches 2026 and 2027 Curriculum Handbook - MCA

into a 3D model and a prototype. Students will use TinkerCAD and / or 
Fusion 360 softwares.

EME 4042 Introduction to Quantum Computers 3 6
This subject explores the emerging opportunities and strategic 
significance of quantum technologies in the context of cybersecurity. It 
collects the latest trends and insights into the evolution of quantum 
computing and quantum communication, offering valuable guidance. To 
this end, it provides comprehensive trends, research, and insights from 
more than 40 leading experts from around the globe to provide a deep 
understanding of the opportunities, challenges, and implications of 
quantum technologies for cyber defence.

revolution, tracing its evolution from traditional human advisors to AI-

technologies are part of a rapidly evolving financial landscape shaped by

driven platforms that democratize financial services. Robo-advisory

B An ability to analyze a problem, and identify and define the computing

inspiring subject that guides students to understand how this new 
technology will empower them to take full advantage of all it has to offer. 
The subject includes fundamental topics and hands-on tutorial exercises 
that students can implement in the hands-on laboratories, and in day to

The subject includes fundamental topics and hands-on tutorial exercises 
that students can implement in the hands-on laboratories, and in day to 
day life and profession. This subject teaches students to get professional 
results out of a desktop 3D printer. Through a series of exercises and case

Fusion 360 softwares.

understanding of the opportunities, challenges, and implications of 
quantum technologies for cyber defence.

EME 4044 AI, FinTech, and the Future of Robo-Advisory 3 6
This subject explores the rise of robo-advisory within the broader FinTech 
revolution, tracing its evolution from traditional human advisors to AI-
driven platforms that democratize financial services. Robo-advisory 
technologies are part of a rapidly evolving financial landscape shaped by 
digitalization, disintermediation, and changing client expectations. 
Emerging trends such as hybrid advisory models, behavioural nudges, 
ethical challenges surrounding algorithmic transparency, and inclusivity 
concerns are also addressed.
Textbook – AI, FinTech, and the Future of 
Robo-Advisory — Risk Management and 
Ethical Considerations by Narmin Nahidi and 
Alex Zarifis, Open Access, Springer Verlag

building. Students will work step-by-step through the game design 
process, starting with only what is necessary to complete each step, and 
refactoring the code as necessary along the way, reflecting the natural

hardware architectures can be leveraged to address the challenges in 
modern data management. This subject furhter presents a wide range of

developments, often related to applications in data analytics or machine 
learning. Students will cover topics such as hardware-accelerated query or 
event processing on FPGA, GPU, and multicore CPUs, scalable data 
management in data center networks or on modern memory and storage 
technologies, and operating system support.

The subject includes fundamental topics and hands-on tutorial exercises

The subject includes fundamental topics and hands-on tutorial exercises

results out of a desktop 3D printer. Through a series of exercises and case

Program Educational Objectives
PEO 1 Our Graduates will have successful professional careers in industry, government, academia
or non-profit organizations.
PEO 2 Our Graduates will effectively lead, work and communicate in multidisciplinary teams and
apply sound engineering principles and design methodology to solve societal problems.
PEO 3 Our Graduates will maintain currency in their chosen field through higher study, through
organizational participation and through participation in professional developmental activi-

PEO 3 Our Graduates will maintain currency in their chosen field through higher study, through
organizational participation and through participation in professional developmental activi-
ties.Admission Batch 2024 Curriculum Handbook - M.Tech in CE (SE)
Program Outcomes
PO 1 Graduates will possess in-depth knowledge of principles and practices in structural engineering, including analysis,
design, and materials used in various structural systems such as buildings, bridges, and other infrastructures.
PO 2 Graduates will be capable of analyzing complex structural problems, employing advanced analytical techniques and
tools to evaluate the performance and safety of structures under various loading conditions.
PO 3 Graduates will demonstrate the ability to design safe, efficient, and sustainable structural systems, considering factors
such as material properties, load factors, and environmental impacts in their designs.

such as material properties, load factors, and environmental impacts in their designs.
PO 4 Graduates will possess the skills to conduct independent research in structural engineering, contributing original
ideas and innovative solutions to contemporary challenges in the field.
PO 5 Graduates will be proficient in using contemporary software and tools for structural analysis, modeling, and design,
ensuring accuracy and efficiency in their engineering solutions.
PO 6 Graduates will have a comprehensive understanding of construction methods, project management principles, and
construction safety, enabling them to oversee structural projects effectively.
PO 7 Graduates will adhere to ethical standards and professional responsibilities, recognizing the importance of safety, sus-
tainability, and societal impact in their engineering practice.
PO 8 Graduates will possess the ability to work collaboratively in multidisciplinary teams, demonstrating leadership in

tainability, and societal impact in their engineering practice.
PO 8 Graduates will possess the ability to work collaboratively in multidisciplinary teams, demonstrating leadership in
managing projects and coordinating efforts among stakeholders.
PO 9 Graduates will be able to communicate complex technical concepts related to structural engineering clearly and effec-
tively to diverse audiences through reports, presentations, and technical documentation.
PO 10 Graduates will acquire project management skills, enabling them to plan, execute, and manage structural engineering
projects efficiently, and will develop an entrepreneurial mindset to lead initiatives in the construction industry.
PO 11 Graduates will recognize the importance of lifelong learning and continuous professional development to stay current
with evolving technologies, materials, and methodologies in structural engineering.

with evolving technologies, materials, and methodologies in structural engineering.
PO 12 Graduates will develop specialized knowledge in emerging areas such as smart materials, advanced construction tech-
niques, seismic design, and sustainable construction practices, positioning them to contribute to future advancements
in structural engineering.Admission Batch 2024 Curriculum Handbook - M.Tech in CE (SE)

GEN 1140 Digital Fabrication Techniques 1 5
Digital Fabrication with 3D Printing is a practical, informative, and inspiring

Publications
Weekly Course Format: 3L - 2P
GEN 1140 Digital Fabrication Techniques 1 5
Digital Fabrication with 3D Printing is a practical, informative, and inspiring 
subject that guides students to understand how this new technology will 
empower them to take full advantage of all it has to offer. The subject 
includes fundamental topics and hands-on tutorial exercises that students 
can implement in the hands-on laboratories, and in day to day life and 
profession. This subject teaches students to get professional results out of a 
desktop 3D printer. Through a series of exercises and case studies, this 
subject gives students the techniques to turn a product idea into a 3D model 
and a prototype. Students will use TinkerCAD and / or Fusion 360 softwares. 
Textbooks 
-Make: Getting Started with 3D Printing 
-Make: Design for 3D Printing 
-Make: The Complete Guide to Tinkercad 
-Make: Fusion 360 for Makers

• Physical Disability:
The admission to B.V.Sc. & A.H. degree course shall not be allowed in the case of candidates suffering
from following disabilities:
• Disability of total body (including chest or spine) more than 50%
• Disability of lower limb of more than 50%
• Disability of Upper limb

• Progressive diseases like myopathies etc.
• Disabilities which otherwise would interfere in the performance of the duties of a veterinarian.

Training
Placement Related Training
Siksha 'O' Anusandhan (Deemed to be University), indulges in systematic activities to promote the 
most important arenas in a students' life in three inclusive parts such as curricular, co-curricular and 
extra-curricular. The systematic activities aim at grooming a student's cognitive, behavioural and 
emotional aspects, offering a matured and industry ready professional personality.
The learning outcome is achieved through various decision-making activities, experiential learning 
and problem-solving techniques. We provide several platforms ranging from language assembly to 
behavioural labs, to catapult the students' life skills development such as initiative, leadership, 
motivation, achievement orientation, interpersonal relations to name a few. The industry looks out for 
students who are vibrant, energetic individuals and ready to accept challenges, attentive, a good

students who are vibrant, energetic individuals and ready to accept challenges, attentive, a good 
academic background, fast learners, open to learning even at work and more importantly, good 
communication skills.
The Training & Placement Cell of SOA mainly focuses on three aspects of career such 
as guiding the students for:
• Placement and subsequent career
• Higher Education
• Entrepreneurship
This career guidance also helps them to go for higher education by being successful in areas of CAT, 
XAT, GMAT, GRE and others.
The Training & Placement Cell of SOA cell takes care of holistic development of 
students with three inclusive activities such as:
• Career Mentoring in form of continuous guidance and Profiling of Students
• Placement Training including:
○ Aptitude Training
○ Reasoning Skills
○ Enhancing Functional Subject Knowledge
○ General Awareness
○ Industry Orientation
○ Soft Skills
○ Group Discussion and Personal Interview Skills

○ Aptitude Training
○ Reasoning Skills
○ Enhancing Functional Subject Knowledge
○ General Awareness
○ Industry Orientation
○ Soft Skills
○ Group Discussion and Personal Interview Skills
• Online Assessments by partnering with the leading assessment partners of India (AMCAT, 
Mettel, Merittrac, Cocubes etc)
The placement training and company specific training module is given in the mode of regular classes 
starting from the pre-final year of all the batches so that they get ready before the start of the campus 
recruitment drives. As SOA does not compromise on quality of delivery, therefore in this endeavour, 
all the leading career development agencies are being involved.

Program Educational Objectives (PEO’s)
• Our Graduates will have successful professional ca reers in industry, government, academia or non-pro fit organizations. 
• Our Graduates will effectively lead, work and comm unicate in multidisciplinary teams and apply sound engineering principles and design methodology to so lve societal 
problems. 
• Our Graduates will maintain currency in their chos en  field through higher study, through organizatio nal participation and through participation in prof essional 
developmental activities.
Program Outcomes (PO’s)
  1. Engineering knowledge: Apply the knowledge of mathematics, science, engineering fundamentals, and  an engineering specialization to the solution of c omplex 
engineering problems.
  2. Problem analysis: Identify, formulate, review research literature, and analyze complex engineerin g problems reaching substantiated conclusions using  first principles of 
mathematics, natural sciences, and engineering scie nces.

Civil Engineering
• By the completion of Civil Engineering program the students will be able to design and build civil engineering based systems in the context of environmental, economical, 
and societal requirements  
• By the completion of Civil Engineering program the students will be able to identify research gaps and hence provide innovative solutions to Civil engineering problems 
and engage in life-long learning for professional growth 
•  By the completion of Civil Engineering program the students will be able to serve the community as ethical and responsible professionals

design of computer-based system of varying complexity.

design of computer-based system of varying complexity.

systems and Power Electronic systems.
